The sand bulges where oversized crabs have buried themselves beneath torn weed and shells. They burst up together when warm feet cross the tidemark.
Dark fins pace the surf beside a line of jagged rocks. The raiders wait for a wave to break hard, then rush through the foam with spears low.
A wounded gull drops into knee-deep water and vanishes in a red flash. The circling shark has learned that panicked splashing brings easier prey.
Shells and weed have arranged themselves into a pattern that repeats along the strand. Reading the tide sign points toward safe ground before the water turns.
A narrow shelf drops away beneath clear water, and the returning wave pulls hard against ankles and packs. Anyone who fights it wrong is dragged across sharp shells.
An abandoned crab pot knocks against a post with each wave. Inside, a waxed purse has tangled in the rope among claws and broken shells.
A column of damp smoke rises from a spit cut off by the tide. Whoever lit it has little time before the next wave front swallows the sandbar.
Tracks from a shuttered beacon lead across wet sand toward sea caves. Without the missing lens, ships on this coast will run blind tonight.
A clutch of large eggs lies half exposed where storm wind stripped the dune grass. Keeping them safe means deciding what deserves protection when the parent returns.
Low thorny vines carry tart berries above the dry sand. The ripe fruit hides under leaves that also conceal small biting insects.
Crunchy saltwort grows where fresh water seeps through the dunes. A careful cut leaves the roots buried and the patch alive.
Long ribbons of kelp have washed ashore after a night storm. The freshest strands must be sorted from spoiled weed before the sun turns them foul.
Clear pools between the rocks hold crabs fattened on trapped minnows. They are easy to see but quick to wedge themselves beneath stone.
Small fish flash just beyond the first break of waves. Casting at the right moment lands a meal before the school moves down the coast.
A flat of wet sand shows tiny breathing holes where clams hide below. Digging fast matters because the returning tide quickly fills every mark.
Green and blue glass glitters among black pebbles at the storm line. The useful pieces are smooth, but broken shards still wait under the sand.
A shallow basin behind the dunes has dried into thick white crust. Lifting clean slabs takes care before damp sand fouls the harvest.
A wave-cut bluff exposes a band of clean red clay. The bank groans with each breaker, so the best clay must be taken quickly and lightly.
Heavy driftwood has lodged high above the tide after last night's storm. It is dry enough to use if freed before the sea reclaims it.
A section of hull lies half buried below the dunes, its nails rusted but its boards still sound. Pulling them free without splitting them takes patient leverage.
A wind-bent palm has dropped a straight, dry trunk after lightning split its crown. It can be cut into usable lengths before salt air softens it.
A storm-damaged pier shudders as something huge hauls itself among the pilings. The merrow wears wreckage like trophies and bellows for tribute from the shore.
Canvas and dune grass hide a low blind aimed at the tideline. The smugglers fire only after the surf covers the sound of bowstrings.
Salt crystals leap from a cracked tide pool and shape themselves into cackling little raiders. Their first burst sends razor flakes across bare skin.
Driftwood stakes form an old tide calendar that only makes sense from the cliff shadow. Reading it reveals when the next safe crossing opens.
A sudden gust whips dry razor shells across the strand. Fast reflexes keep exposed hands and faces from being cut bloody.
Three blue pieces of sea glass form a Harper trail mark beside a cracked shell. Someone passed this way in a hurry and expected friendly eyes.
A lighthouse lens lies wrapped in sailcloth below a dune cut, still warm from recent handling. Returning it before nightfall could keep ships from the rocks.
Bootprints and drag marks lead from the missing lens toward a sea cave that floods at high tide. Whoever made them knew the tide table too well.
A frightened deckhand waves from a barnacled rock that will vanish under the next tide. They know who hired the lens thieves if rescued in time.
A blue lantern blinks from a headland where no beacon should burn. Stopping the false light means crossing exposed beach under watching eyes.
A wreck bell rings from the surf though no ship is visible. Following the sound finds the wreckers' last trap before another vessel answers.
Purple dune plums grow where fresh water hides under the sand. The ripe fruit is half-buried and bruises if pulled too quickly.
Peppery greens grow just beyond the salt line. Cutting the tender tops leaves enough root for the patch to survive.
Round kelp bulbs wash up only on the low moon tide. The good ones sound hollow and sweet when tapped.
The surf boils with silver sardines fleeing something larger offshore. A fast cast fills a basket before the school breaks.
Broad rock crabs wedge themselves into a basalt crack below the spray. Hooking them out takes patience before the tide returns.
Oysters gleam in a shallow bed exposed only under moonlight. The largest shells cling hard to stone and cut careless hands.
Black sand gathers in stripes below a cliff of dark stone. A magnet or careful pan separates useful grit from ordinary beach sand.
A waxy lump bobs among weed and gull feathers. It is valuable if identified before someone tosses it back as refuse.
A chalk bluff has cracked after rain, dropping clean blocks above the tide. Harvesting them means working before the cliff sheds more.
A carved figurehead rolls in the breakers, still attached to sound oak beams. Pulling it free takes timing with the waves.
An old dune fence has collapsed under blown sand. Several weathered rails remain dry and useful if dug out intact.
A salt-stunted pine leans over the bluff, dead but solid. Cutting it without sending the trunk into the surf is the trick.
